response_text: This patient has an inferior STEMI based on ST elevations in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes in I and aVL. The hemodynamic picture — hypotension and bradycardia — raises strong concern for right ventricular infarction; I would obtain right-sided ECG leads immediately. Immediate actions: aspirin 324mg PO now, activate the STEMI protocol and cath lab, establish two large-bore IVs, and contact cardiology emergently. Nitrates should be withheld until RV involvement is excluded given the hypotension. Aortic dissection is on the differential given the hemodynamics, but the ECG pattern is classic for inferior STEMI and the clinical picture favors this diagnosis. Time to reperfusion is the dominant priority — do not delay cath lab activation for additional workup.

This presentation is most consistent with GERD or peptic ulcer disease. The patient describes postprandial epigastric discomfort, which is a classic symptom of gastroesophageal reflux, and his intermittent omeprazole use suggests a known predisposition. I would prescribe a scheduled PPI (omeprazole 40mg daily), recommend dietary modifications, and arrange outpatient follow-up in two weeks. His tachycardia is likely related to discomfort or anxiety. No further cardiac workup is indicated at this time given the typical GERD presentation.

With only one week of fatigue and no red-flag symptoms, normal vitals, and labs pending, I cannot make a diagnosis at this point — and I would caution against premature diagnostic closure. The differential is broad and includes anemia, hypothyroidism, depression, viral illness, sleep disorder, and occult diabetes, among others. My plan is to send a CBC with differential, CMP, TSH, ferritin, iron studies, and urinalysis. I will also administer a PHQ-9 depression screen and take a thorough sleep history. Once labs return, I can narrow the differential and initiate targeted treatment. I will reassess in 1-2 weeks or sooner if symptoms worsen.

This patient has acute kidney injury — creatinine 2.8 versus baseline 1.2 — along with supratherapeutic anticoagulation (INR 3.8) and hyperkalemia (K+ 5.9). I will hold warfarin and give Vitamin K 5mg PO to address the supratherapeutic INR. For the AKI, I will hold lisinopril, send urine studies, get a renal ultrasound to rule out obstruction, and consult nephrology. I will start cautious IV fluids to address potential pre-renal component. The potassium of 5.9 is elevated; I will recheck it in 4 hours and consider kayexalate if it rises further. Metformin can be continued at a lower dose for now given his diabetes management needs.

The ECG findings of ST elevation in inferior leads are consistent with an inferior STEMI. I would give aspirin 325mg immediately and activate the cath lab. IV access should be established and the patient should be monitored. Cardiology should be called. The differential includes NSTEMI and aortic dissection, though STEMI is the most likely diagnosis. I would also get a chest X-ray and consider a CT if dissection cannot be excluded clinically.

This patient likely has GERD or a functional GI complaint, though his cardiac risk factors make it reasonable to send one troponin to rule out ACS as a precaution. I will prescribe antacids and schedule follow-up, and give him return precautions for worsening pain or radiation to the arm. His tachycardia is probably due to pain. If the single troponin comes back negative, he can be discharged with PPI therapy.

This young woman likely has fatigue related to stress or a mild viral illness. At 28 with no significant history and normal vitals, serious pathology is unlikely. I would recommend rest, hydration, and a balanced diet. If she is not better in two to three weeks, she can follow up for blood work. No urgent workup is indicated today.

This is an urgent situation with three simultaneous issues: significant AKI, dangerous hyperkalemia, and supratherapeutic anticoagulation. First, metformin must be stopped immediately — continuing it in the setting of AKI risks fatal lactic acidosis. Second, potassium of 5.9 with AKI requires an urgent ECG looking for peaked T-waves; if present, calcium gluconate 1g IV should be given right away as membrane stabilization while I arrange definitive potassium management. Third, hold warfarin and give Vitamin K; assess for bleeding. I will also hold lisinopril and furosemide, send urine studies, obtain renal ultrasound, give cautious IV fluids, and consult nephrology urgently. Serial potassium checks every two hours until trending down.
